text: In 2018, a team of researchers at Case Western Reserve University found that merely having a smartphone within eyesight — even face-down and silenced — measurably reduced available working-memory capacity. They called this 'brain drain': the device does not need to interrupt you to cost you cognitive resources. Its presence alone activates the habit of checking, drawing a thin but persistent thread of attention toward it. The effect was strongest in participants who reported the highest smartphone dependence, suggesting a feedback loop: the more tethered you feel, the more the device taxes you simply by existing nearby.
question: What did the Case Western study find about silenced smartphones left in plain sight?

options.text: They caused no measurable distraction when notifications were disabled
They reduced working-memory capacity through the habit of anticipatory checking
They increased anxiety primarily in users who also used social media frequently
They disrupted sleep quality but had no effect on daytime cognitive performance
